FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, 9-11 year-olds from 21-site US study, baseline survey/interview data

FT_IC2.evidence: Electronic media use via Screen Time Questionnaire: TV, videos, video games, texting, social networking, video chat. Each reported separately with own coefficient. Applies B1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized linear mixed models, regression coefficients for EMU predicting child anxiety and depression controlling for depression/anxiety respectively

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports coefficients, t-values, 95% CIs for anxiety and depression outcomes with EMU predictors

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining differential relationships of anxiety and depression to child and parent report of electronic media use

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of preprint/abstract/duplicate; original peer-reviewed publication

FT_EC7_reason: Electronic media/SM are independent variables predicting anxiety/depression outcomes, not dependent variables. Analysis is SM→outcome, not outcome→SM. Does not satisfy condition (a) of FT-EC7.
notes: Paper demonstrates differential effects of specific EM types on anxiety vs depression, with video gaming and chatting associated with anxiety while video watching related to depression.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study baseline (2016-18), 9-10 year-olds, cross-sectional data from broadly US representative sample (n=11,875)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time types (television/movies, videos, video games, social media) measured via Youth Screen Time Survey with separate coefficients per type and content. Mature-rated video games and R-rated movies also assessed. Applies B1 (separate β per ST type/content).

FT_IC3.evidence: Negative binomial structural equation modeling with regression coefficients and mediation/indirect effects testing. Direct effects in Table 2, indirect effects in Table 3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Incidence rate ratios (IRRs), 95% confidence intervals, p-values, and β coefficients reported in Tables 2 and 3 for both direct and indirect effects

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining associations between ST types/content and problem behaviors, with sleep duration as mediator

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate; original peer-reviewed publication in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2019)

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/social media are independent variables predicting problem behaviors (externalizing/internalizing outcomes), not dependent variables. This is ST→outcome analysis, not outcome→SM structure required for FT-EC7.
notes: Comprehensive analysis of 4 ST types + 2 content categories predicting 8 CBCL syndrome scales. Sleep mediated effects are small but significant for several ST-behavior associations.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD first annual curated release, cross-sectional baseline, 4524 children ages 8-11 years, US study

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as recreational ST (≤2 hours/day guideline recommendation) but NOT reported with separate qualifying coefficients. ST is bundled into combined movement behavior recommendation categories (PA+ST+sleep, ST+sleep, etc.). No individual ST coefficient reported independently. Violates B1 requirement for separate per-item breakdown. Screen time appears only as component of aggregate behavior recommendation compliance, not as separately analyzed qualifying variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: FT-IC2 not met; criterion 3 not evaluated

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC2 not met; criterion 4 not evaluated

FT_IC5.evidence: FT-IC2 not met; criterion 5 not evaluated

FT_IC6.evidence: FT-IC2 not met; criterion 6 not evaluated

FT_EC7_reason: Paper screened out at FT-IC2; FT-EC7 not evaluated
notes: Screen time treated as part of 24-hour movement guideline compliance (combined PA+ST+sleep recommendations) rather than as separately-analyzed smartphone/SM variable. Tables 3-4 report paths for movement behavior combinations and impulsivity dimensions, but ST has no independent coefficient. Applies B2: summed/total behavior recommendation without per-item breakdown.
